annotations.text: A travel history might add to an already long differential diagnosis, as would a history of medication use or visits to a traditional health worker, which commonly precede a visit to a nurse or doctor.
The first question a local clinician would ask may seem startling to an outsider in view of this patient's relatively young age: Does this 19-year-old woman already have established HIV-1 infection?
A large study involving South African adolescents suggests that 13.8% of 19-year-old women tested positive for HIV, and more than 30% tested positive by the age of 21 years.
The presence of a genital ulcer means that this patient has a high likelihood of HIV infection.
If a specimen contains HIV antibodies, the antibodies will bind to the test antigens and trigger the detection system to generate a positive result.

The diagnostic hallmarks of acute HIV infection are a negative test for HIV-1 antibodies on EIA, an indeterminate or negative test for HIV-1 on Western blot analysis, and detection of HIV-1 RNA in plasma.
This patient was tested for HIV-1 infection at the first clinic with the use of rapid tests on whole blood
The patient's geographic and social demographic profile makes HIV infection a major issue to consider.
She epitomizes the challenges facing young women in a country where HIV is endemic. With le
A systematic review of 37 randomized, controlled trials involving HIV prevention has revealed that only 5 showed successful HIV prevention.
Although it may seem surprising that our patient, with a total of only four sexual encounters, has acute HIV infection, her situation is all too common in South Africa.
The difference in sexual coupling patterns, in terms of age and sex, and the high prevalence of HIV, continue to drive the southern African epidemic.

Girls from 15 through 19 years of age have a higher rate of HIV infection, by a factor of 3 to 6, than their male counterparts, even though first sexual contact occurs at similar ages; nearly peak HIV prevalence in women is reached by the age of 20 years.

The Centre for the AIDS Programme of Research in South Africa (CAPRISA) recently announced the results from the CAPRISA 004 study, a phase 2B, randomized, controlled trial of 1% tenofovir gel that showed a 39% reduction in HIV acquisition in women and a 51% reduction in the acquisition of herpes simplex virus type 2.
Although interventions regarding prevention have consistently shown a significant effect, these efforts in care and treatment programs unfortunately remain small or nonexistent.
The South African government recently launched a substantive campaign to promote knowledge of HIV status and a program for medical male circumcision.
Treatment of the sexually transmitted disease may decrease the plasma viral load, which may slow the progression to AIDS.
antiretroviral treatment of acute HIV infection is controversial, with no firm evidence of clinical benefit, although at least one study suggests immunologic benefit.
the patient requires intensive counseling regarding safe-sex practices.
At the time of her last visit, 1 year after the onset of her illness, her CD4+ T-cell count was 514 cells per cubic millimeter and her plasma HIV-1 RNA load was 30,644 copies per milliliter.
